topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification blinked on my screen, a stark, unwelcome intrusion into my quiet evening. My stomach clenched, a tight knot forming beneath my ribs. I clicked it, my fingers trembling slightly as I read the headline. My piece, the one I’d poured months of late nights into, was featured prominently, but the author listed was completely unfamiliar. A cold wash spread through my limbs, making the air in my small apartment feel suddenly thin. I reread the byline, the unfamiliar name echoing hollowly in the silence. I stood up abruptly, pacing the worn rug, my breath catching in short, shallow gasps. Who was this person? How could this have happened? A wave of profound disorientation washed over me, leaving me rooted to the spot, staring blankly at the ceiling as the reality of the situation began to settle, heavy and undeniable.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, the carefully constructed composure I’d maintained all day crumbling around the edges. My jaw tightened so hard I could feel the muscle jump beneath my skin. It wasn't just a mistake; it felt like a deliberate erasure. A strange, prickly heat rose up my neck, and I instinctively reached up to touch my collar, seeking some anchor in the sudden swirling chaos. Every word of the article, my unique perspective, was now tethered to another identity. A slow, creeping dread began to coil in my chest, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I walked to the window, the city lights blurring through my suddenly unfocused vision. The world outside seemed too loud, too sharp, and I felt profoundly disconnected, as if I were watching a performance of my own life from a distance.

[story 3]

When I saw the attribution, the blood seemed to drain from my face. My hands, which had been resting calmly on the keyboard moments before, clenched into fists, the knuckles whitening. A sharp, involuntary shudder ran through my frame. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning in protest beneath me, and forced myself to look again, scrutinizing the text. The discrepancy was undeniable, a glaring error that somehow felt monumental. A hollow echo resonated in my ears, a sound devoid of warmth. I stood up slowly, my movements stiff, as if moving through water. I walked to the door, my heart hammering a frantic rhythm against my ribs, every instinct screaming that something fundamental had shifted, something deeply wrong with the foundation of what I thought was real.
